content.problemStatement:
Chef has a binary grid of size M×NM \times NM×N filled with zeros and ones. Chef is interested in finding "perfect" square regions within this grid.

A **square submatrix** is a contiguous square-shaped section of the grid. Chef wants to count how many such square submatrices consist **entirely of ones**.

Help Chef find the total count of all such square submatrices (of any size 1×1,2×2,…1 \times 1, 2 \times 2, \dots1×1,2×2,…) that contain only 111s.

Function Declaration

**Function Name**



countSquareSubmatricescountSquareSubmatricescountSquareSubmatrices



**Parameters**



matrixmatrixmatrix: A 2D list/vector of integers representing the binary grid (000s and 111s).



**Return Value**



Returns an integerintegerinteger representing the total count of square submatrices composed entirely of ones.


Constraints
1≤T≤101 \leq T \leq 101≤T≤10
1≤M,N≤3001 \leq M, N \leq 3001≤M,N≤300
matrix[i][j]∈{0,1}matrix[i][j] \in \{0, 1\}matrix[i][j]∈{0,1}
Input Format
The first line contains a single integer TTT — the number of test cases.
For each test case:

The first line contains two integers MMM and NNN — the dimensions of the matrix.
The next MMM lines contain NNN space-separated integers (000 or 111) representing each row.


Output Format
For each test case, print a single integer — the total number of square submatrices within Chef's binary matrix that contain only ones.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains a single integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains two integers M and N — the dimensions of the matrix. The next M lines contain N space-separated integers (0 or 1) describing the matrix.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single integer — the total number of square submatrices that consist entirely of ones.

sampleTestCases.explanation: All cells are 1. Number of 1×1 squares = 9, 2×2 squares = 4, 3×3 squares = 1. Total = 14.
Only the two individual cells containing 1 form valid squares.
A single cell of 1 forms exactly one 1×1 square.

solution.approach: The problem can be solved using dynamic programming. We create a 2D array dp where dp[i][j] represents the size of the largest square submatrix ending at position (i, j) with all elements as 1. We initialize dp[i][j] to 1 if the current element is 1 and it's the first row or column. Otherwise, we set dp[i][j] to the minimum of dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1], and dp[i-1][j-1] plus 1. We then add the sum of the first 'dp[i][j]' natural numbers to our answer for each position (i, j).
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * M * N) per test case, O(T * M * N) total.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(M * N) — for the dp array.
